  - link "on Jul 16, 2026on Jul 16, 2026":
    - /url: https://github.com/Martinmjal/sales_ai_agent_case_study/issues/1#issue-4897479390
  - button "Issue body actions":
  - heading "Problem Statement" [level=2]
  - paragraph: The project currently has a small mock agent that can execute and score one AutomationBench sales task, but it does not provide the evaluator-facing experience needed to understand an execution end to end.
  - paragraph: The current command-line entry point selects one task, blocks until execution finishes, and writes a latest-result JSON file that is overwritten by the next default run. There is no durable execution history, no live trace, no browser-independent run lifecycle, no way to select any registered sales task, and no visual relationship between the task prompt, available tools, assistant turns, tool calls, tool results, final response, deterministic score, assertion results, and world-state changes.
  - paragraph: This makes it unnecessarily difficult for an evaluator or agent developer to inspect why an agent passed, partially passed, failed, stopped, or crashed. It also couples the current experience to the temporary LangGraph mock agent even though the final Challenge agent will use a custom loop.
  - heading "Solution" [level=2]
  - paragraph: Build a local, single-user Agent UI as a root-level sibling application that wraps the current mock agent through a framework-neutral runtime contract.
  - paragraph: "The application will provide a minimalist, ChatGPT/Claude-like three-pane workspace:"
  - list:
    - listitem: A collapsible execution-history drawer on the left.
    - listitem: A calm task-selection and high-level progress surface in the center.
    - listitem: A complete evaluator inspector on the right.
  - paragraph: Any of the 100 registered AutomationBench sales tasks will be runnable by canonical task ID. Exactly one execution may be active at a time. Execution will be owned by the server, not the browser, so refreshes, navigation, and disconnected clients do not stop the agent. The only runtime control in the UI will be a graceful Stop action.
  - paragraph: Every execution will be preserved as one complete, self-contained, root-level session JSON artifact. Running artifacts will be updated atomically as events occur and become immutable when terminal. The right inspector will reconstruct a live vertical causal timeline from normalized events and show the prompt, tools, final response, score, assertion details, usage, timing, errors, and initial-to-final world-state diff.
  - paragraph: "The current mock agent will receive only compatibility upgrades: dynamic task lookup, asynchronous event streaming, cooperative cancellation, structured outcomes, and richer observable metadata. The UI will not add agent reasoning, retries, steering, or recovery behavior. A future custom agent will replace the mock-agent adapter without requiring changes to the API, frontend, or persisted session schema."
  - heading "User Stories" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to browse every registered AutomationBench sales task, so that I can choose the exact benchmark problem I want to inspect.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tasks identified by their canonical benchmark IDs, so that the UI matches AutomationBench terminology and filtering behavior.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want task names humanized without hiding canonical IDs, so that the picker is readable and precise.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to search tasks by name or canonical ID, so that I can quickly locate one task among the full sales dataset.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to preview the user prompt, tool count, and assertion count before starting, so that I understand the task’s shape.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want every registered sales task to remain runnable, so that UI policy does not create an artificial supported subset.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want exactly one execution active at a time, so that local resources and session ownership remain unambiguous.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the configured model, maximum steps, and agent version recorded rather than editable in the UI, so that results remain reproducible.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a clear empty state before a run starts, so that the first action is obvious.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want concise high-level progress in the center, so that I can follow the run without duplicating detailed tool activity.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want detailed tool activity confined to the right inspector, so that the chat-like center remains calm and readable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to see the selected task prompt as the opening interaction, so that the execution context is immediately clear.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to see the agent’s final response prominently, so that I can compare what it claimed with what it actually accomplished.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want execution to continue after I refresh the browser, so that browser state cannot accidentally terminate a benchmark.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want execution to continue when I browse historical sessions, so that inspection does not affect the active run.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a visible Return to active run affordance, so that I can quickly return after inspecting history.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to reopen the application and recover the current running trace, so that reconnecting does not lose progress.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want recovered running nodes to retain their running presentation, so that the interface accurately reflects current state after refresh.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want live events to appear without duplicates after reconnection, so that the causal trace remains trustworthy.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a single Stop execution control, so that I can deliberately end a long-running task.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want Stop to take effect at a safe model or tool-batch boundary, so that the trace and world state remain coherent.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a stopped session to preserve its partial trace and current score when scoring is available, so that incomplete work remains inspectable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a server-killed run marked Interrupted on restart, so that it is not incorrectly presented as still running.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want interrupted runs never replayed automatically, so that completed tool side effects are not duplicated.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a collapsible left history drawer, so that I can maximize workspace while retaining access to previous executions.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want history grouped by Today, Previous 7 days, and Older, so that sessions remain navigable over time.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to search history by humanized task name or canonical ID, so that I can find a prior execution quickly.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want status and score visible in each history item, so that I can identify interesting runs at a glance.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the selected history session to drive both the center and right panels, so that information from different executions is never mixed.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the right inspector to show a sticky summary of status, partial credit, strict pass/fail, duration, and tokens, so that the outcome remains visible while I inspect details.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the task prompt and available tool definitions preserved in the inspector, so that I can judge whether the agent had the necessary context and capabilities.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want assistant turns arranged on a vertical causal spine, so that I can follow execution from start to finish.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want parallel tool calls to branch from their initiating assistant turn, so that actual concurrency is represented rather than flattened.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tool results to reconnect before the next assistant turn, so that cause and effect are visually traceable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tool arguments, results, durations, and errors inspectable, so that I can diagnose incorrect actions.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want running, completed, stopped, interrupted, and failed nodes to be visually distinct, so that lifecycle state is unambiguous.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the deterministic score broken down assertion by assertion, so that partial credit is explainable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want strict pass/fail shown separately from partial credit, so that the two benchmark signals are not conflated.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want excluded or pre-satisfied assertions represented accurately, so that assertion totals are not misleading.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to compare initial and final world state through a human-readable diff, so that I can see exactly what changed.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the full initial and final world snapshots available, so that a result remains reproducible if benchmark source changes.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the complete raw session JSON available behind a collapsed disclosure, so that no diagnostic evidence is hidden.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want provider-visible reasoning summaries and reasoning-token counts preserved when available, so that observable reasoning metadata is not discarded.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the UI never to invent or expose hidden chain-of-thought, so that the trace remains honest and provider-compliant.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tool-error events displayed exactly as produced by the runtime, so that the UI does not obscure failures.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the UI to avoid injecting recovery instructions or retrying failed operations, so that evaluation measures the agent rather than the interface.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want terminal sessions immutable, so that scores and evidence cannot silently change after completion.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want every session stored in a repository-visible folder, so that execution evidence can be reviewed and committed.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want sessions to be self-contained rather than indexes into mutable benchmark code, so that historic executions remain meaningful.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the session writer to survive browser disconnections, so that persistence is part of runtime lifecycle rather than presentation lifecycle.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the current CLI to remain usable, so that adding the UI does not remove the existing workflow.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the mock agent’s fresh world and task-specific tool binding preserved, so that UI integration does not change benchmark semantics.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want official AutomationBench scoring preserved, so that scores match the benchmark rather than a UI reimplementation.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the Agent UI isolated from LangGraph-specific message types, so that the temporary framework can be removed later.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the future custom agent to implement the same runtime contract, so that replacing the mock agent does not require rebuilding the evaluator.
    - listitem: As a maintainer, I want AutomationBench treated as an unmodified external dependency, so that the case-study application remains separable from the benchmark repository.
    - listitem: As a maintainer, I want a single local startup command, so that evaluators can run the interface without a frontend build workflow.
    - listitem: As a maintainer, I want no CDN dependencies, so that the local interface does not depend on third-party asset availability.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a warm-neutral and low-noise visual treatment, so that dense execution evidence remains readable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the application to remain usable on narrower screens through drawers, so that all evidence is accessible without requiring a fixed wide viewport.
    - listitem: As a keyboard user, I want task selection, history navigation, disclosures, and Stop accessible without a mouse, so that core evaluation workflows are operable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want clear unavailable states when scoring or a final response cannot be produced, so that missing data is not mistaken for zero or empty success.
  - heading "Implementation Decisions" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: The Agent UI will be a root-level sibling application named Agent-UI. Its Python project configuration will live with the application so it does not claim packaging decisions needed by the future final agent.
    - listitem: The Agent UI will use FastAPI for the local API and server lifecycle, with framework-free HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for presentation.
    - listitem: The application is local and single-user. It will run as one server process with one active execution, no authentication, database, distributed queue, or multi-worker coordination.
    - listitem: The FastAPI layer will own HTTP and browser concerns. The mock agent will not depend on FastAPI.
    - listitem: The Agent UI will depend on a framework-neutral runtime contract that accepts a benchmark task, frozen execution configuration, event sink, and cancellation signal, and returns a structured execution result.
    - listitem: A mock-agent runtime adapter will translate LangGraph events into the normalized runtime contract. The future custom agent will replace this adapter.
    - listitem: AutomationBench remains an unmodified external dependency.
    - listitem: Task discovery will come from the registered sales dataset, not reflection over every Python task factory. Canonical task IDs are the stable UI and API identifiers.
    - listitem: All 100 registered sales tasks will be selectable and runnable. The six unregistered sales task factories will not be exposed merely because they exist in source.
    - listitem: Dataset information that is serialized as JSON will be normalized at the task-catalog boundary.
    - listitem: The mock agent will gain dynamic task resolution, asynchronous event streaming, cooperative cancellation, structured terminal outcomes, and richer observable metadata.
    - listitem: Compatibility work will not change the mock agent’s prompts, reasoning policy, tool-recovery behavior, official scorer, fresh world creation, or task-declared tool binding.
    - listitem: The existing command-line workflow will remain supported as a separate consumer of the runtime.
    - listitem: The normalized event contract will use monotonically increasing sequence IDs, timestamps, lifecycle kind, run and parent identifiers when available, message and tool-call identifiers, content, tool inputs/results/errors, usage, duration, and metadata.
    - listitem: Provider-visible reasoning summaries or content blocks may be preserved. Hidden chain-of-thought will not be requested, inferred, or represented.
    - listitem: The server will own execution in a background task. Request cancellation, browser refresh, navigation, or SSE disconnection will not cancel execution.
    - listitem: Exactly one execution may have Running status. Attempts to start another will receive a clear conflict response.
    - listitem: Cooperative Stop will be checked between model nodes or completed tool batches. It will preserve the current trace, world, and partial evaluation when scoring is possible.
    - listitem: No steering, pause/resume, same-session continuation, frontend-injected recovery, or frontend-added retries will exist.
    - listitem: Tool errors will be recorded and displayed. Whether execution continues after an error is solely the runtime’s behavior.
    - listitem: The session lifecycle includes Running, Completed, Stopped, Failed, and Interrupted. Terminal sessions are immutable.
    - listitem: On startup, persisted Running sessions without a live in-process owner will transition to Interrupted. They will not automatically resume or replay.
    - listitem: One self-contained JSON artifact will represent one benchmark execution. A session will never contain a batch or multiple executions.
    - listitem: Session artifacts will live in the repository-root sessions directory, which will be explicitly trackable and will not be added to ignore rules.
    - listitem: Session filenames will combine creation time, task slug, and a short unique identifier.
    - listitem: Each artifact will include schema version; session identity, timestamps, status, and terminal error; frozen task identity and prompt; assertions and initial state; agent configuration and available tool definitions; normalized ordered events; explicit final response; partial and strict evaluation with assertion results; token and timing usage; and initial state, end state, and diff.
    - listitem: Session files will be rewritten atomically after meaningful lifecycle events. The materialized JSON file is the source of truth for history and reconnect recovery.
    - listitem: The browser will reconstruct session state from the materialized artifact, then subscribe to reconnectable SSE events.
    - listitem: SSE reconnection will use event sequence IDs to avoid gaps and duplicates.
    - listitem: The public API will support task listing, session creation, session-list summaries, complete session reads, session event subscription, and Stop.
    - listitem: History selection will be client application state. Session IDs will not be encoded into browser routes in this version.
    - listitem: The selected session will be the single source of truth for both center and right panels.
    - listitem: The left history drawer will be collapsible, searchable, grouped by Today, Previous 7 days, and Older, and show task, canonical ID, timestamp, status, and score.
    - listitem: The center will show task selection before execution, then high-level live progress, Stop while Running, and the final response when terminal. It will not duplicate detailed tools.
    - listitem: The right inspector will be a continuous scroll with a sticky score summary followed by prompt, tool definitions, live causal timeline, final response, assertion breakdown, world-state diff, and collapsed raw JSON.
    - listitem: Assistant turns will form the vertical trace spine. Parallel tool calls will branch from the initiating turn and reconnect before the next assistant turn.
    - listitem: Score and assertion results remain pending until completion or Stop. Failed sessions show evaluation only when the runtime can safely produce it.
    - listitem: Model, maximum steps, and agent version are server-side configuration. They are frozen into the session and displayed but are not editable in the UI.
    - listitem: The visual treatment will be light-only, warm-neutral, minimalist, and desktop-first. Wide layouts use three columns; narrower layouts use collapsible rails or overlay drawers.
    - listitem: The application will use local assets and system typography, with no frontend build pipeline or CDN dependency.
  - heading "Testing Decisions" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: Tests will assert externally observable behavior rather than private implementation details such as internal callback classes, CSS selectors unrelated to behavior, or LangGraph event internals.
    - listitem: The primary and highest testing seam will be the Agent UI’s public HTTP/SSE API exercised with the scripted mock-agent runtime. This one seam covers runtime adaptation, lifecycle ownership, persistence, scoring, and client reconnection without an external model call.
    - listitem: Lower-level tests will be added only where fault injection is impractical through the public seam, particularly atomic replacement and startup recovery.
    - listitem: "Existing mock-agent tests provide prior art: a scripted runnable drives the real graph, tool node, world mutation, and official scorer end to end without an API call."
    - listitem: Existing task-tool binding and official scoring tests will remain passing after compatibility upgrades.
    - listitem: Task-catalog tests will verify that exactly the registered sales dataset is exposed, canonical IDs resolve correctly, and serialized task information is normalized.
    - listitem: Runtime contract tests will verify ordered model and tool events, parallel tool-call correlation, visible tool errors, explicit final response, structured usage, and terminal outcomes.
    - listitem: API tests will verify task listing, session creation, single-active conflict behavior, session summaries, complete artifact reads, and Stop semantics.
    - listitem: Background-lifecycle tests will verify that disconnecting or abandoning the initiating request does not terminate execution.
    - listitem: SSE tests will disconnect and reconnect using a prior sequence ID, then verify no missing or duplicated events.
    - listitem: Persistence tests will verify that each meaningful event produces a valid materialized artifact, that terminal artifacts reject mutation, and that session filenames are unique and repository-visible.
    - listitem: Startup-recovery tests will seed a Running artifact without a live owner and verify transition to Interrupted without replay.
    - listitem: Cancellation tests will use a controllable scripted runtime to request Stop during a model call and tool batch, then verify safe-boundary termination and preserved partial evidence.
    - listitem: Failure tests will verify that runtime-produced tool errors are displayed and that fatal errors close the session without UI-generated retries or guidance.
    - listitem: Evaluation tests will verify partial credit, strict pass, assertion status, excluded assertions, final world, and world diff against the official scorer’s output.
    - listitem: Browser-level functional tests will verify task selection, history selection, active-run navigation, Stop visibility, disclosures, and session synchronization between all three panes.
    - listitem: Visual QA will inspect wide and narrow layouts, long prompts, parallel tool branches, large tool results, failures, empty history, active execution, and completed evaluation states.
    - listitem: Automated tests will use scripted local execution and temporary session directories. They will not require an OpenAI API key or mutate committed session artifacts.
  - heading "Out of Scope" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: Building the final custom agent required by the Challenge.
    - listitem: Changing the mock agent’s planning, prompts, context management, reasoning, retry, or recovery behavior.
    - listitem: Agent steering or free-text user messages before, during, or after execution.
    - listitem: Pause/resume, same-session recovery, or resuming a process-killed execution.
    - listitem: Multiple executions within one session.
    - listitem: More than one concurrently active execution.
    - listitem: Batch evaluation, run comparison, experiment grouping, or aggregate charts.
    - listitem: A rerun shortcut on terminal sessions; repeating a task uses the normal task picker.
    - listitem: Editing, renaming, or deleting session history from the UI.
    - listitem: User-selectable model, effort, maximum steps, toolset, or agent configuration.
    - listitem: Multi-user deployment, authentication, authorization, hosted infrastructure, databases, worker queues, or distributed coordination.
    - listitem: Modifying or extending the AutomationBench repository or its existing visualizer.
    - listitem: Exposing the selected session through URL routing or deep links.
    - listitem: Dark mode or a phone-first redesign.
    - listitem: Hidden chain-of-thought capture or fabricated reasoning nodes.
    - listitem: Automatic redaction, retention policies, or production handling of non-synthetic sensitive tool data.
    - listitem: Automatic replay of tool calls after interruption.
  - heading "Further Notes" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: The Challenge prohibits agent frameworks for the final agent. LangGraph remains temporary only to prove benchmark integration; the Agent UI boundary is intentionally framework-neutral.
    - listitem: Observability is explicitly valuable to the Challenge evaluation, so the trace, deterministic score, and world-state evidence are core functionality rather than decorative dashboard features.
    - listitem: The current mock runner already creates a fresh world, binds task-declared tools, serializes messages and tool calls, applies official scoring, and returns final state. Compatibility work should build on these seams instead of replacing them.
    - listitem: The current default result path is repository-visible but overwrites the prior execution. The new sessions directory changes this into durable append-only history.
    - listitem: AutomationBench’s visualizer run directories are unsuitable for this feature because their schema differs and their JSON output locations are ignored.
    - listitem: "Full benchmark snapshots are small enough for the local tracked-artifact design: the 100 sales initial states total only a few megabytes."
    - listitem: The Agent UI is an evaluator and observability surface. Its behavior must never make an agent appear more capable than the runtime actually is.

  - button "Issue body actions":
  - heading "Problem Statement" [level=2]
  - paragraph: The project currently has a small mock agent that can execute and score one AutomationBench sales task, but it does not provide the evaluator-facing experience needed to understand an execution end to end.
  - paragraph: The current command-line entry point selects one task, blocks until execution finishes, and writes a latest-result JSON file that is overwritten by the next default run. There is no durable execution history, no live trace, no browser-independent run lifecycle, no way to select any registered sales task, and no visual relationship between the task prompt, available tools, assistant turns, tool calls, tool results, final response, deterministic score, assertion results, and world-state changes.
  - paragraph: This makes it unnecessarily difficult for an evaluator or agent developer to inspect why an agent passed, partially passed, failed, stopped, or crashed. It also couples the current experience to the temporary LangGraph mock agent even though the final Challenge agent will use a custom loop.
  - heading "Solution" [level=2]
  - paragraph: Build a local, single-user Agent UI as a root-level sibling application that wraps the current mock agent through a framework-neutral runtime contract.
  - paragraph: "The application will provide a minimalist, ChatGPT/Claude-like three-pane workspace:"
  - list:
    - listitem: A collapsible execution-history drawer on the left.
    - listitem: A calm task-selection and high-level progress surface in the center.
    - listitem: A complete evaluator inspector on the right.
  - paragraph: Any of the 100 registered AutomationBench sales tasks will be runnable by canonical task ID. Exactly one execution may be active at a time. Execution will be owned by the server, not the browser, so refreshes, navigation, and disconnected clients do not stop the agent. The only runtime control in the UI will be a graceful Stop action.
  - paragraph: Every execution will be preserved as one complete, self-contained, root-level session JSON artifact. Running artifacts will be updated atomically as events occur and become immutable when terminal. The right inspector will reconstruct a live vertical causal timeline from normalized events and show the prompt, tools, final response, score, assertion details, usage, timing, errors, and initial-to-final world-state diff.
  - paragraph: "The current mock agent will receive only compatibility upgrades: dynamic task lookup, asynchronous event streaming, cooperative cancellation, structured outcomes, and richer observable metadata. The UI will not add agent reasoning, retries, steering, or recovery behavior. A future custom agent will replace the mock-agent adapter without requiring changes to the API, frontend, or persisted session schema."
  - heading "User Stories" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to browse every registered AutomationBench sales task, so that I can choose the exact benchmark problem I want to inspect.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tasks identified by their canonical benchmark IDs, so that the UI matches AutomationBench terminology and filtering behavior.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want task names humanized without hiding canonical IDs, so that the picker is readable and precise.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to search tasks by name or canonical ID, so that I can quickly locate one task among the full sales dataset.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to preview the user prompt, tool count, and assertion count before starting, so that I understand the task’s shape.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want every registered sales task to remain runnable, so that UI policy does not create an artificial supported subset.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want exactly one execution active at a time, so that local resources and session ownership remain unambiguous.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the configured model, maximum steps, and agent version recorded rather than editable in the UI, so that results remain reproducible.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a clear empty state before a run starts, so that the first action is obvious.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want concise high-level progress in the center, so that I can follow the run without duplicating detailed tool activity.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want detailed tool activity confined to the right inspector, so that the chat-like center remains calm and readable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to see the selected task prompt as the opening interaction, so that the execution context is immediately clear.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to see the agent’s final response prominently, so that I can compare what it claimed with what it actually accomplished.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want execution to continue after I refresh the browser, so that browser state cannot accidentally terminate a benchmark.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want execution to continue when I browse historical sessions, so that inspection does not affect the active run.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a visible Return to active run affordance, so that I can quickly return after inspecting history.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to reopen the application and recover the current running trace, so that reconnecting does not lose progress.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want recovered running nodes to retain their running presentation, so that the interface accurately reflects current state after refresh.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want live events to appear without duplicates after reconnection, so that the causal trace remains trustworthy.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a single Stop execution control, so that I can deliberately end a long-running task.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want Stop to take effect at a safe model or tool-batch boundary, so that the trace and world state remain coherent.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a stopped session to preserve its partial trace and current score when scoring is available, so that incomplete work remains inspectable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a server-killed run marked Interrupted on restart, so that it is not incorrectly presented as still running.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want interrupted runs never replayed automatically, so that completed tool side effects are not duplicated.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a collapsible left history drawer, so that I can maximize workspace while retaining access to previous executions.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want history grouped by Today, Previous 7 days, and Older, so that sessions remain navigable over time.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to search history by humanized task name or canonical ID, so that I can find a prior execution quickly.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want status and score visible in each history item, so that I can identify interesting runs at a glance.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the selected history session to drive both the center and right panels, so that information from different executions is never mixed.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the right inspector to show a sticky summary of status, partial credit, strict pass/fail, duration, and tokens, so that the outcome remains visible while I inspect details.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the task prompt and available tool definitions preserved in the inspector, so that I can judge whether the agent had the necessary context and capabilities.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want assistant turns arranged on a vertical causal spine, so that I can follow execution from start to finish.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want parallel tool calls to branch from their initiating assistant turn, so that actual concurrency is represented rather than flattened.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tool results to reconnect before the next assistant turn, so that cause and effect are visually traceable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tool arguments, results, durations, and errors inspectable, so that I can diagnose incorrect actions.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want running, completed, stopped, interrupted, and failed nodes to be visually distinct, so that lifecycle state is unambiguous.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the deterministic score broken down assertion by assertion, so that partial credit is explainable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want strict pass/fail shown separately from partial credit, so that the two benchmark signals are not conflated.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want excluded or pre-satisfied assertions represented accurately, so that assertion totals are not misleading.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want to compare initial and final world state through a human-readable diff, so that I can see exactly what changed.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the full initial and final world snapshots available, so that a result remains reproducible if benchmark source changes.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the complete raw session JSON available behind a collapsed disclosure, so that no diagnostic evidence is hidden.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want provider-visible reasoning summaries and reasoning-token counts preserved when available, so that observable reasoning metadata is not discarded.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the UI never to invent or expose hidden chain-of-thought, so that the trace remains honest and provider-compliant.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want tool-error events displayed exactly as produced by the runtime, so that the UI does not obscure failures.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the UI to avoid injecting recovery instructions or retrying failed operations, so that evaluation measures the agent rather than the interface.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want terminal sessions immutable, so that scores and evidence cannot silently change after completion.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want every session stored in a repository-visible folder, so that execution evidence can be reviewed and committed.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want sessions to be self-contained rather than indexes into mutable benchmark code, so that historic executions remain meaningful.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the session writer to survive browser disconnections, so that persistence is part of runtime lifecycle rather than presentation lifecycle.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the current CLI to remain usable, so that adding the UI does not remove the existing workflow.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the mock agent’s fresh world and task-specific tool binding preserved, so that UI integration does not change benchmark semantics.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want official AutomationBench scoring preserved, so that scores match the benchmark rather than a UI reimplementation.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the Agent UI isolated from LangGraph-specific message types, so that the temporary framework can be removed later.
    - listitem: As an agent developer, I want the future custom agent to implement the same runtime contract, so that replacing the mock agent does not require rebuilding the evaluator.
    - listitem: As a maintainer, I want AutomationBench treated as an unmodified external dependency, so that the case-study application remains separable from the benchmark repository.
    - listitem: As a maintainer, I want a single local startup command, so that evaluators can run the interface without a frontend build workflow.
    - listitem: As a maintainer, I want no CDN dependencies, so that the local interface does not depend on third-party asset availability.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want a warm-neutral and low-noise visual treatment, so that dense execution evidence remains readable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want the application to remain usable on narrower screens through drawers, so that all evidence is accessible without requiring a fixed wide viewport.
    - listitem: As a keyboard user, I want task selection, history navigation, disclosures, and Stop accessible without a mouse, so that core evaluation workflows are operable.
    - listitem: As an evaluator, I want clear unavailable states when scoring or a final response cannot be produced, so that missing data is not mistaken for zero or empty success.
  - heading "Implementation Decisions" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: The Agent UI will be a root-level sibling application named Agent-UI. Its Python project configuration will live with the application so it does not claim packaging decisions needed by the future final agent.
    - listitem: The Agent UI will use FastAPI for the local API and server lifecycle, with framework-free HTML, CSS, and JavaScript for presentation.
    - listitem: The application is local and single-user. It will run as one server process with one active execution, no authentication, database, distributed queue, or multi-worker coordination.
    - listitem: The FastAPI layer will own HTTP and browser concerns. The mock agent will not depend on FastAPI.
    - listitem: The Agent UI will depend on a framework-neutral runtime contract that accepts a benchmark task, frozen execution configuration, event sink, and cancellation signal, and returns a structured execution result.
    - listitem: A mock-agent runtime adapter will translate LangGraph events into the normalized runtime contract. The future custom agent will replace this adapter.
    - listitem: AutomationBench remains an unmodified external dependency.
    - listitem: Task discovery will come from the registered sales dataset, not reflection over every Python task factory. Canonical task IDs are the stable UI and API identifiers.
    - listitem: All 100 registered sales tasks will be selectable and runnable. The six unregistered sales task factories will not be exposed merely because they exist in source.
    - listitem: Dataset information that is serialized as JSON will be normalized at the task-catalog boundary.
    - listitem: The mock agent will gain dynamic task resolution, asynchronous event streaming, cooperative cancellation, structured terminal outcomes, and richer observable metadata.
    - listitem: Compatibility work will not change the mock agent’s prompts, reasoning policy, tool-recovery behavior, official scorer, fresh world creation, or task-declared tool binding.
    - listitem: The existing command-line workflow will remain supported as a separate consumer of the runtime.
    - listitem: The normalized event contract will use monotonically increasing sequence IDs, timestamps, lifecycle kind, run and parent identifiers when available, message and tool-call identifiers, content, tool inputs/results/errors, usage, duration, and metadata.
    - listitem: Provider-visible reasoning summaries or content blocks may be preserved. Hidden chain-of-thought will not be requested, inferred, or represented.
    - listitem: The server will own execution in a background task. Request cancellation, browser refresh, navigation, or SSE disconnection will not cancel execution.
    - listitem: Exactly one execution may have Running status. Attempts to start another will receive a clear conflict response.
    - listitem: Cooperative Stop will be checked between model nodes or completed tool batches. It will preserve the current trace, world, and partial evaluation when scoring is possible.
    - listitem: No steering, pause/resume, same-session continuation, frontend-injected recovery, or frontend-added retries will exist.
    - listitem: Tool errors will be recorded and displayed. Whether execution continues after an error is solely the runtime’s behavior.
    - listitem: The session lifecycle includes Running, Completed, Stopped, Failed, and Interrupted. Terminal sessions are immutable.
    - listitem: On startup, persisted Running sessions without a live in-process owner will transition to Interrupted. They will not automatically resume or replay.
    - listitem: One self-contained JSON artifact will represent one benchmark execution. A session will never contain a batch or multiple executions.
    - listitem: Session artifacts will live in the repository-root sessions directory, which will be explicitly trackable and will not be added to ignore rules.
    - listitem: Session filenames will combine creation time, task slug, and a short unique identifier.
    - listitem: Each artifact will include schema version; session identity, timestamps, status, and terminal error; frozen task identity and prompt; assertions and initial state; agent configuration and available tool definitions; normalized ordered events; explicit final response; partial and strict evaluation with assertion results; token and timing usage; and initial state, end state, and diff.
    - listitem: Session files will be rewritten atomically after meaningful lifecycle events. The materialized JSON file is the source of truth for history and reconnect recovery.
    - listitem: The browser will reconstruct session state from the materialized artifact, then subscribe to reconnectable SSE events.
    - listitem: SSE reconnection will use event sequence IDs to avoid gaps and duplicates.
    - listitem: The public API will support task listing, session creation, session-list summaries, complete session reads, session event subscription, and Stop.
    - listitem: History selection will be client application state. Session IDs will not be encoded into browser routes in this version.
    - listitem: The selected session will be the single source of truth for both center and right panels.
    - listitem: The left history drawer will be collapsible, searchable, grouped by Today, Previous 7 days, and Older, and show task, canonical ID, timestamp, status, and score.
    - listitem: The center will show task selection before execution, then high-level live progress, Stop while Running, and the final response when terminal. It will not duplicate detailed tools.
    - listitem: The right inspector will be a continuous scroll with a sticky score summary followed by prompt, tool definitions, live causal timeline, final response, assertion breakdown, world-state diff, and collapsed raw JSON.
    - listitem: Assistant turns will form the vertical trace spine. Parallel tool calls will branch from the initiating turn and reconnect before the next assistant turn.
    - listitem: Score and assertion results remain pending until completion or Stop. Failed sessions show evaluation only when the runtime can safely produce it.
    - listitem: Model, maximum steps, and agent version are server-side configuration. They are frozen into the session and displayed but are not editable in the UI.
    - listitem: The visual treatment will be light-only, warm-neutral, minimalist, and desktop-first. Wide layouts use three columns; narrower layouts use collapsible rails or overlay drawers.
    - listitem: The application will use local assets and system typography, with no frontend build pipeline or CDN dependency.
  - heading "Testing Decisions" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: Tests will assert externally observable behavior rather than private implementation details such as internal callback classes, CSS selectors unrelated to behavior, or LangGraph event internals.
    - listitem: The primary and highest testing seam will be the Agent UI’s public HTTP/SSE API exercised with the scripted mock-agent runtime. This one seam covers runtime adaptation, lifecycle ownership, persistence, scoring, and client reconnection without an external model call.
    - listitem: Lower-level tests will be added only where fault injection is impractical through the public seam, particularly atomic replacement and startup recovery.
    - listitem: "Existing mock-agent tests provide prior art: a scripted runnable drives the real graph, tool node, world mutation, and official scorer end to end without an API call."
    - listitem: Existing task-tool binding and official scoring tests will remain passing after compatibility upgrades.
    - listitem: Task-catalog tests will verify that exactly the registered sales dataset is exposed, canonical IDs resolve correctly, and serialized task information is normalized.
    - listitem: Runtime contract tests will verify ordered model and tool events, parallel tool-call correlation, visible tool errors, explicit final response, structured usage, and terminal outcomes.
    - listitem: API tests will verify task listing, session creation, single-active conflict behavior, session summaries, complete artifact reads, and Stop semantics.
    - listitem: Background-lifecycle tests will verify that disconnecting or abandoning the initiating request does not terminate execution.
    - listitem: SSE tests will disconnect and reconnect using a prior sequence ID, then verify no missing or duplicated events.
    - listitem: Persistence tests will verify that each meaningful event produces a valid materialized artifact, that terminal artifacts reject mutation, and that session filenames are unique and repository-visible.
    - listitem: Startup-recovery tests will seed a Running artifact without a live owner and verify transition to Interrupted without replay.
    - listitem: Cancellation tests will use a controllable scripted runtime to request Stop during a model call and tool batch, then verify safe-boundary termination and preserved partial evidence.
    - listitem: Failure tests will verify that runtime-produced tool errors are displayed and that fatal errors close the session without UI-generated retries or guidance.
    - listitem: Evaluation tests will verify partial credit, strict pass, assertion status, excluded assertions, final world, and world diff against the official scorer’s output.
    - listitem: Browser-level functional tests will verify task selection, history selection, active-run navigation, Stop visibility, disclosures, and session synchronization between all three panes.
    - listitem: Visual QA will inspect wide and narrow layouts, long prompts, parallel tool branches, large tool results, failures, empty history, active execution, and completed evaluation states.
    - listitem: Automated tests will use scripted local execution and temporary session directories. They will not require an OpenAI API key or mutate committed session artifacts.
  - heading "Out of Scope" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: Building the final custom agent required by the Challenge.
    - listitem: Changing the mock agent’s planning, prompts, context management, reasoning, retry, or recovery behavior.
    - listitem: Agent steering or free-text user messages before, during, or after execution.
    - listitem: Pause/resume, same-session recovery, or resuming a process-killed execution.
    - listitem: Multiple executions within one session.
    - listitem: More than one concurrently active execution.
    - listitem: Batch evaluation, run comparison, experiment grouping, or aggregate charts.
    - listitem: A rerun shortcut on terminal sessions; repeating a task uses the normal task picker.
    - listitem: Editing, renaming, or deleting session history from the UI.
    - listitem: User-selectable model, effort, maximum steps, toolset, or agent configuration.
    - listitem: Multi-user deployment, authentication, authorization, hosted infrastructure, databases, worker queues, or distributed coordination.
    - listitem: Modifying or extending the AutomationBench repository or its existing visualizer.
    - listitem: Exposing the selected session through URL routing or deep links.
    - listitem: Dark mode or a phone-first redesign.
    - listitem: Hidden chain-of-thought capture or fabricated reasoning nodes.
    - listitem: Automatic redaction, retention policies, or production handling of non-synthetic sensitive tool data.
    - listitem: Automatic replay of tool calls after interruption.
  - heading "Further Notes" [level=2]
  - list:
    - listitem: The Challenge prohibits agent frameworks for the final agent. LangGraph remains temporary only to prove benchmark integration; the Agent UI boundary is intentionally framework-neutral.
    - listitem: Observability is explicitly valuable to the Challenge evaluation, so the trace, deterministic score, and world-state evidence are core functionality rather than decorative dashboard features.
    - listitem: The current mock runner already creates a fresh world, binds task-declared tools, serializes messages and tool calls, applies official scoring, and returns final state. Compatibility work should build on these seams instead of replacing them.
    - listitem: The current default result path is repository-visible but overwrites the prior execution. The new sessions directory changes this into durable append-only history.
    - listitem: AutomationBench’s visualizer run directories are unsuitable for this feature because their schema differs and their JSON output locations are ignored.
    - listitem: "Full benchmark snapshots are small enough for the local tracked-artifact design: the 100 sales initial states total only a few megabytes."
    - listitem: The Agent UI is an evaluator and observability surface. Its behavior must never make an agent appear more capable than the runtime actually is.

payload.input:
const r = await tools.mcp__node_repl__js({title:"Inspect sub-issue controls",code:`var subUiText = await ticketTab.playwright.evaluate(() => Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('dialog,[role="dialog"],[role="menu"],[role="listbox"]')).slice(0,10).map(el => ({role: el.getAttribute("role"), text: (el.innerText || "").slice(0,2000)})));
nodeRepl.write(subUiText);`}); for (const c of (r?.content ?? [])) { if (c.type === "image") image(c); else if (c.type === "text") text(c.text); }

var subUiText = await ticketTab.playwright.evaluate(() => Array.from(document.querySelectorAll('dialog,[role="dialog"],[role="menu"],[role="listbox"]')).slice(0,10).map(el => ({role: el.getAttribute("role"), text: (el.innerText || "").slice(0,2000)})));
nodeRepl.write(subUiText);
